suggestions.title: Alternative 1: Current Approach — Synchronous Data Pipeline
suggestions.overview: The current design uses a synchronous data pipeline where data flows through processing stages sequentially. Each stage completes before the next begins, providing simple error handling and immediate consistency.
suggestions.what_changes: No changes — this describes the existing design as-is.
advantages: Simple to reason about and debug
Immediate consistency — no eventual consistency concerns
disadvantages: Single point of failure in the pipeline
No horizontal scalability for processing stages
suggestions.description: The current synchronous pipeline processes data sequentially through each stage.

trade_offs.description: Simple sequential flow is easy to debug

suggestions.title: Alternative 2: Event-Driven Data Pipeline
suggestions.overview: Replace the synchronous data pipeline with an event-driven architecture using message queues. Each processing stage publishes events that downstream stages consume asynchronously, enabling independent scaling and fault isolation.
suggestions.what_changes: Add a message broker (e.g., SQS or RabbitMQ) between processing stages. Convert each stage to an independent consumer. Add dead-letter queues for failed messages. Replace direct function calls with event publishing.
advantages: Better fault tolerance and natural dead-letter handling
Stages can be deployed and scaled independently
disadvantages: Eventual consistency instead of immediate processing
More difficult to debug end-to-end flows
suggestions.complexity_justification: Requires new infrastructure (message broker), event schema design, and consumer implementation for each stage.
suggestions.description: Replace the synchronous data pipeline with an event-driven architecture using message queues.

trade_offs.description: Better fault tolerance and natural dead-letter handling
Eventual consistency instead of immediate processing

suggestions.title: Alternative 3: OAuth2 Provider Abstraction
suggestions.overview: Implement an OAuth2 provider abstraction layer supporting multiple identity providers. This decouples authentication logic from the core application and allows swapping providers without code changes.
suggestions.what_changes: Add an AuthProvider interface with implementations for each provider (e.g., Cognito, Auth0, Keycloak). Move JWT validation behind the abstraction. Add provider configuration to the config file.
advantages: Supports multiple auth providers without code changes
Easier to test with mock providers
disadvantages: May over-abstract if only one provider is ever needed
suggestions.complexity_justification: Requires interface design and at least one concrete implementation, but no new infrastructure.
suggestions.description: Implement an OAuth2 provider abstraction layer supporting multiple identity providers.

trade_offs.description: Supports multiple auth providers without code changes

recommendation: Alternative 2 (Event-Driven Data Pipeline) is recommended for this project. The critique findings highlight significant risks around synchronous coupling and cascading failures, which the event-driven approach directly addresses. While it adds infrastructure complexity, the improved fault tolerance and scalability align with the project's reliability requirements. The current approach (Alternative 1) should be used as a baseline for comparison during implementation planning.
